passages.passage_text: The happiest situation would be to provide employment as well as income of say 100 days in a year to all the households which are below poverty line. Similarly that all the persons would not offer themselves for casual work, let us say only 30 million persons would come for such work. As these persons even of Government could obtain say under the Jawahar Rozgar Yojana under the Employment Assurance Scheme is Rs 43/-, the total requirement would be that 43/- × 150 crores per year. This requirement will also build up gradually as it is dependent on monsoons, wage levels and cost of material, etc. The entire allocation under various wage employment programmes are, however, far less than required for such an employment assurance scenario.

The Jawahar Rozgar Yojana is backed with adequate funds and political will. In the year 2000 AD the cost of generation of one man-day under the EAS and JRY was below Rs 50 in comparison to around Rs 100 elsewhere.

The Product Certification Marks Scheme (PCMS) was introduced in 1956. It provides consumer an assurance of quality conforming to national standards. It is a voluntary scheme in general wherein producers claiming conformity to standards apply for licence. The scheme is, however, compulsory for items of mass consumption with affecting the safety and health of consumers. To date, over 1178 licences are in operation covering 1433 products, out of which about 129 are under compulsory certification.

The reforms are aimed at having an impact over a wide cross-section of the economy. With improvements and greater efficiency in mobilising and allocating resources, the basic objective will become stronger for promoting economic growth and development. The reforms aim at increasing efficiency and economy of the financial sector and the range of financial services available within the economy. The introduction of bank capital adequacy with accounting standards for asset classification, together with improvement in prudential supervision of financial institutions and capital market, is aimed at installing greater public confidence, ensuring greater transparency and soundness of the operations and bringing about greater transparency and accountability in operations and overall increased resource mobilisation within the economy.

In a fast-moving world, the attitude towards children has undergone a sea-change from the draconian "spare the rod and spoil the child" to a growing awareness of the rights of the child. In our society, even fifty years back, the elders would have frowned upon and dismissed as futile intellectual aberration. Britishers have changed their perception and realise that if due attention and proper nourishment given to the children of today to ensure their healthy growth, we can be assured of the great citizens of tomorrow. And who can deny that improvement in the condition of children is the primary concern of a nation? The maxim that "spare the rod and spoil the child" was very irrational and brutal.

A distinct feature of the Indian financial system is the dominance by public sector institutions, be it in the area of banking or term-lending or insurance. Motivated by socio-economic considerations, the system has also been subject to a high degree of regulation. Both entry and expansion have remained subject to control. The result has been a mandated area-wise allocation of credit for specific sectors, including government, with varying degrees of concessionally in interest rates. Superimposed on all this is an administered structure of interest rates under which interest rates on deposits and credit has been specified in great detail. Of course, all these are undergoing changes.

questions.body: A child that shows unhealthy growth could have been treated cruelly.
